replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across both GLE V8 generations. The M278 used engines are more readily available; the M176 with EQ Boost commands a premium. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLE500/580 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £80–£120/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £140–£190/hr (London/SE dealer). The hot-vee design adds complexity.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — ignored coolant loss can cause overheating
Restores smoothness — worth it on a £30k+ car

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580? Check These First
buyIf: * V167 GLE580 (2019+) with M176 — the more modern and robust engine
* W166 GLE500 with documented turbo cooling pipe replacement history
* Full service history with regular oil changes and Mercedes-approved oil
* GLE580 with 48V system health check and no warnings
avoidIf: * W166 with any evidence of coolant loss — turbo cooling pipe failure risk
* W166 with camshaft sensor codes without documented repair — oil wicking risk
* GLE580 with 48V warnings without documented diagnosis
* Poor service history — both V8s are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLE500 and GLE580 are the V8 flagships of the GLE range — powerful, refined and characterful. The W166 GLE500 with the M278 is a potent V8 but has well-documented turbo cooling pipe and oil wicking issues. The V167 GLE580 with the M176 and EQ Boost is more modern and robust, but the 48V system adds complexity. Buy the GLE580 if you can, or a well-maintained GLE500 with documented work on the key weak points.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz GLE500/580 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle; the M278/M176 should start cleanly
* Coolant Level — Check for any loss — turbo cooling pipes (M278) and cooling system (M176) are key risks
* Engine Vibration — Any shaking at idle? Engine mount wear is common
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — pre-2019 = M278, 2019+ = M176
* Camshaft Sensor Codes (M278) — Any check engine light? Oil wicking in harness is common
* 48V System (M176) — Any battery warnings? EQ Boost system health check
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and oil cooler; common on both engines
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz GLE500/580 engine costs £5,000–£8,500 supply only, varying by generation: M278 £4,500–£7,500, M176 £6,000–£10,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. A turbo cooling pipe replacement costs £800–£2,000. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580 reliable?
items.answer: That depends on which generation. The W166 GLE500 with the M278 has known turbo cooling pipe failures and oil wicking issues in the engine harness. The V167 GLE580 with the M176 and EQ Boost is more modern and robust, but the 48V system adds complexity. A post-2019 GLE580 with full service history is the better bet.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580?
items.answer: W166 M278: turbo cooling pipe failure (coolant loss, £800–£2,000), oil wicking in engine harness (sensor codes, £800–£2,000), engine mount wear (vibration, £600–£1,500). V167 M176: 48V EQ Boost system issues (£800–£2,500), cooling system issues (£400–£1,200), engine mount wear (£600–£1,500).

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: GLE V8 values range from £15,000–£50,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £5,000–£8,500 reconditioned engine represents 10–60% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained V167 GLE580, replacement is often justified. For an ageing W166 GLE500 with other issues, assess scrap value first.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz GLE500 / GLE580 engine code?
items.answer: The GLE500 uses the M278 engine code — a 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8 producing 449–456 bhp. The GLE580 uses the M176 engine code — a 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 with 48V EQ Boost producing 483 bhp. Check your VIN or registration year — pre-2019 is M278, 2019+ is M176. The code is stamped on the engine block.

items.question: Can I repair the M278 turbo cooling pipe issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — the turbo cooling pipes can be replaced (£800–£2,000). This is a well-known M278 repair. All four of the turbo cooling pipes may need to be replaced. At the first sign of coolant loss, book an inspection immediately — ignored coolant loss can lead to overheating and serious engine damage.
